summary: McKenzie shares Jonathan Coulton's 'Code Monkey' song as the unofficial anthem of his software business, finding humor and inspiration in its depiction of programmer life. The post is a lighthearted interlude in his business blog, but he slips in a sharp observation about pricing psychology: despite loving the song, he never paid for it because it was free, illustrating how free pricing signals low value and discourages voluntary payment. He notes this lesson is relevant to the micro-ISV community debating pricing strategies.
key_points: McKenzie identifies deeply with the 'Code Monkey' song as capturing the indie developer experience
He makes a pointed observation about his own behavior: he loved the song but never paid because it was free, demonstrating that free pricing undermines voluntary payment
He frames this as a cautionary lesson for micro-ISV developers who underprice or give away their software
The post shows McKenzie's early ability to extract business insights from everyday experiences
money_quotes: I try not to listen to it when actually coding because the tears of laughter make it hard to see semicolons.
After one listen to it I would have gladly paid money for it. However, since its free, I haven't taken time to send the guy so much as a $1 Paypal thanking him for his time.
I think there is a lesson here for a segment of the uISV community regarding pricing-as-signal.
key_insight: When you price something at free, even people who genuinely love it and would have paid will default to paying nothing — price is a signal, not just a number.
